turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I saw a push method that seems to copy whole array each time!
It doesn’t copy every element—it grows the underlying buffer only when it fills up, so most pushes are O(1).

Your code only checks the fixed size, so it will overflow after four pushes. But in a proper dynamic array you allocate more space when full—e.g., doubling the buffer. Then pushing fits within allocated memory most of the time.
What does O(1) per operation mean? My code above seems to allocate new memory every push.
O(1) means constant‑time on average, not that we never touch memory. Doubling amortized cost is 2n for n elements, so each element is moved at most once over many operations.
Why not just use a linked list instead? No reallocation needed.
What's the difference between chaining and open addressing in hash maps?

Chaining is like using linked lists for collisions—simple to add but can get long; open addressing puts items directly into an array—fast lookups if there’s empty slots, but you need a good hash function.
Which approach feels more natural when the load factor spikes?
Are you working in Python or Java for this map?
I'm doing it in Python with the built‑in dict and a custom hash.
Seems like your hash might be causing keys to keep landing in the same slot after resizing, leading to an infinite loop.
Here’s my map class... (code) why is it crashing? "
The issue is the modulo in your rehash function; you’re using (i % size) which stays zero for some values, causing all items into bucket 0. Use (i ^ size) & (size-1).
Thanks! That makes sense.
Let me know if you want a full refactor.
The average‑case O(n log n) comes from the fact that, on random input, each partition splits roughly in half. The cost of one level is O(n), and there are about log₂n levels.
But when I run it with a list of 10 000 integers I get 'RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded'.

That happens because your pivot choice can degenerate the partition, creating very unbalanced sub‑arrays. For a worst‑case input like already sorted data, quicksort degrades to O(n²) and also hits Python’s recursion limit.
So average case is still O(log n) depth even if I use last element? Or does the randomness guarantee that?
The expected depth is O(log n) because with a random pivot the probability of extremely unbalanced partitions decays exponentially.
But my code still seems to recurse heavily for sorted input. Is there any way to avoid recursion altogether?
You can convert it iteratively or increase recursion limit, but the underlying algorithmic cost is what matters: O(n log n) average.
